coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon dots derived from covalent organic frameworks embedded in molecularly imprinted polymers for optosensing of flonicamid
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Xinyue
Liu, Huilin
Sun, Baoguo
coredata.dc:description: After applied, flonicamid reaches the interior of agricultural products and shifts to ecosystem, which proliferating the hazardous risk to consumers. In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from covalent organic frameworks embedded in molecularly imprinted polymers (COF-derived NCDs@MIPs) were prepared by reverse microemulsion polymerization. The COF-derived NCDs@MIPs were used as a potential candidate for the optosensing of flonicamid in food samples, which combined the merits of superior physicochemical properties with the high stability of N-doped carbon dots, an efficient fluorescence-resonance charge-transfer strategy, and good specific and sensitive MIP technology. The performance of the proposed optosensing system was measured in phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH = 7.0) over the flonicamid concentration range of 0.03–0.3 μg g−1 with a limit of detection of 0.004 μg g−1. A selectivity study was employed to evaluate the influence of five neonicotinoids on the selectivity of the COF-derived NCDs@MIPs for flonicamid molecules, which showed good affinity for flonicamid in comparison with that of the non-imprinted polymer (NIP). The application of the proposed optosensing technique in spiked food samples revealed a good recovery (86.7–98.1%). The development of easily synthesized COF-derived NCDs@MIPs with excellent performance will be greatly beneficial for trace detection of flonicamid.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Flonicamid
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Optosensing
N-doped carbon dots
Covalent organic frameworks

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of NCDs from layered COFs
Analysis of vegetable and fruit samples
Preparation and characterization of NCDs and COF-derived NCDs@MIPs
Effect of solvent of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs
Effect of dose of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs
Effect of pH on COF-derived NCDs@MIPs
Specificity of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs for FLO
Calibration curve of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs for FLO
Detection of FLO based on COF-derived NCDs@MIPs in food samples

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31822040), the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2018YFC1602300), the Young Top-Notch Talent of High-Level Innovation and Entrepreneurs Support Program (2017000026833ZK28), the School Level Cultivation Fund of Beijing Technology and Business University for Distinguished and Excellent Young Scholars (BTBUYP2020), and Postgraduate Cultivation of Beijing Technology and Business University-The Improvement Plan of Postgraduate Education Quality (19008020144).

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31822040 ), the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2018YFC1602300 ), the Young Top-Notch Talent of High-Level Innovation and Entrepreneurs Support Program (2017000026833ZK28), the School Level Cultivation Fund of Beijing Technology and Business University for Distinguished and Excellent Young Scholars (BTBUYP2020), and Postgraduate Cultivation of Beijing Technology and Business University-The Improvement Plan of Postgraduate Education Quality (19008020144).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthesis of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs.

(a) HRTEM characterization of NCDs, (b) SEM characterization of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs, (c) size distribution histogram of NCDs, (d) size distribution histogram of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs, (e) FT-IR spectra of NCDs and COF-derived NCDs@MIPs, and (f) UV–vis spectra of FLO and fluorescence spectra of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs.

(a) Effect of dispersion solvent on COF-derived NCDs@MIPs, (b) effect of adsorption solvent on COF-derived NCDs@MIPs, (c) effect of NCD concentration on fluorescence response, (d) effect of NCD concentration on quenching constant, (e) effect of dosage of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs, and (f) effect of pH on COF-derived NCDs@MIPs.
(a) Specificity of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs for FLO and its analogues and (b) the quenching constant of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs and COF-derived NCDs@NIPs for FLO and its analogues.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs with FLO concentrations of 0.03–0.30 μg g−1. Insert: The fluorescence responses of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs obtained with different FLO concentrations. (b) The fluorescence spectra of COF-derived NCDs@NIPs with different FLO concentrations.
Detection and recoveries of FLO from spiked vegetables and fruits.

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon dots derived from covalent organic frameworks embedded in molecularly imprinted polymers for optosensing of flonicamid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health, Beijing Engineering and Technology Research Center of Food Additives, Beijing Technology and Business University, No. 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing 100048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health
Beijing Engineering and Technology Research Center of Food Additives

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health, Beijing Engineering and Technology Research Center of Food Additives, Beijing Technology and Business University, No. 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing 100048, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: After applied, flonicamid reaches the interior of agricultural products and shifts to ecosystem, which proliferating the hazardous risk to consumers. In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from covalent organic frameworks embedded in molecularly imprinted polymers (COF-derived NCDs@MIPs) were prepared by reverse microemulsion polymerization. The COF-derived NCDs@MIPs were used as a potential candidate for the optosensing of flonicamid in food samples, which combined the merits of superior physicochemical properties with the high stability of N-doped carbon dots, an efficient fluorescence-resonance charge-transfer strategy, and good specific and sensitive MIP technology. The performance of the proposed optosensing system was measured in phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH = 7.0) over the flonicamid concentration range of 0.03–0.3 μg g−1 with a limit of detection of 0.004 μg g−1. A selectivity study was employed to evaluate the influence of five neonicotinoids on the selectivity of the COF-derived NCDs@MIPs for flonicamid molecules, which showed good affinity for flonicamid in comparison with that of the non-imprinted polymer (NIP). The application of the proposed optosensing technique in spiked food samples revealed a good recovery (86.7–98.1%). The development of easily synthesized COF-derived NCDs@MIPs with excellent performance will be greatly beneficial for trace detection of flonicamid.

ce:section.ce:para: Access to food safety is fundamental to human survival and the development of well-being, and pesticide residue control is indicated as a key priority in food safety. Flonicamid (FLO), N-cyanomethyl-4-trifluoromethylnicotinamide, has been widely used as a selective systemic pesticide that is highly effective against aphids and other sucking insects since 2000 years [1]. FLO residues in food naturally pose a potential hazardous risk to consumers. The maximum FLO residue limits in foods have been regulated by the US at 0.20–16 mg/kg, and the EU limits are more stringent, i.e., 0.05–2.0 mg/kg [2]. Therefore, selective and sensitive methods for the separation and detection of FLO in food samples are required. High-pressure liquid chromatography (HPLC) coupled with mass spectrometry (MS) is the most reported method for the detection of FLO and its metabolites in food and agricultural samples [2–5]. However, in some ways, adequate FLO residue detection is hindered by the lack of HPLC-MS instruments for analytical measurements and the price of these detection tools.

It is well known that the advantage of molecularly imprinted technology (MIT) is that it is relatively inexpensive and simple to use and can be reused many times to directly reduce the cost of detection. The resulting molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are obtained by polymerizing functional monomers, cross-linking, and template molecules around the porogen. Once polymerization has taken place, the template molecules are extracted from the MIPs to form a highly cross-linked, three-dimensional network polymer [6]. The network holes are specific binding sites with shapes, sizes and functionalities complementary to the template molecules, yielding a preference for other closely related compounds. Owing to the advantages of high selectivity and low cost, MIPs have been served as effective absorbents [7], recognition elements of nanosensor via combined with optosensing [8], electrochemical [9–12] and surface plasmon resonance [13].

Optosensing technology uses optical signals as a means to perceive, extract, transfer and calibrate various kinds of information to be measured, with the advantages of portability, rapidity, and possible onsite use. The optical information of a sample is obtained in real time as a result of the interaction between the sensor and chemicals by recognition and signal treatment [14]. It is important to point out that fluorescent carbon nanomaterials are often used as optical response elements due to their superiority in terms of resistance to photobleaching, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, low cost, and the abundance of their raw materials in nature [15–18]. Fluorescent carbon nanodots (C-dots) have attracted much attention for bioimaging, photosensing, light-emitting diodes, and lasers because of the numerous functional groups on their surface and can be readily produced on a large scale by many methods [19–24]. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) can efficiently induce charge delocalization and tune the work function, efficiently promoting the electron transfer capability of carbon-based materials, and can further extend absorption in the visible light region [25,26]. Therefore, nitrogen doping can be constructed and introduced to the carbon-based fluorescence sensing system for enhanced photo-performance.

Another effective way to improve the photo-performance is a critical search for good synthesis methods and carbon sources for NCDs with improved fluorescence efficiency. It is well known that complex procedures and strong acid treatment are often required during NCD preparation [27]. However, this processing leads to the production of NCDs that suffer from low photoluminescence emission. The well-defined crystalline porous structures of covalent organic frameworks (COFs) are ingeniously constructed with organic building units via strong covalent bonds [28]. COFs can be used as candidates for the preparation of NCDs with tailored functionalities. The optical properties of NCDs can be tailored by selectively choosing various organic molecules as COFs.

In this study, we used a facile and general method to prepare COF-derived NCDs@MIPs in a reverse microemulsion system of Triton X-100 and cyclohexane for one-pot polymerization using a silane reagent for functionalization. The NCDs were derived from COFs and obtained a remarkable fluorescence intensity because of changes in the electronic structure and charge delocalization. The COF-derived NCDs@MIPs were used as a fluorescence probe for FLO optosensing in vegetable and fruit samples. After extraction to remove the FLO molecules, a series of three-dimensional cavities were created on the surface of the COF-derived NCDs@MIPs with matched size, structure, and functional groups for FLO repeatability. Detailed mechanisms of the fluorescence quenching between COF-derived NCDs@MIPs and FLO molecules were systematically investigated.

The standards of FLO, thiamethoxam (THIA), thiacloprid (THI), imidacloprid (IMI), and nitenpyram (NIT) were provided by Sigma–Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Triton X-100 and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cyclohexane was provided by Fuchen Chemical Regent Factory (Tianjin, China). 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) was obtained from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China). Double deionized water (DDW, 18.2 MΩ cm−1) was prepared using a Water Pro water purification system (Labconco, Kansas City, MO). All other reagents were of at least analytical grade and purchased from Beijing Chemicals (Beijing, China).

The morphologies were obtained by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SU1510, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (2010 FEF microscope, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan), respectively. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) using a Nicolet iS10 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was performed in the range of 800–4000 cm−1. Fluorescence spectra were acquired using a multifunctional microplate reader (Biotek Synergy H1, Biotek Instruments Inc., Winooski, VT).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NCDs from layered COFs
ce:section.ce:para: The NCDs were prepared according to a previously reported method with slight modifications [29]. Typically, 1 g of 1,4-dicyanobenzene monomer and 1.06 g of ZnCl2 were added to an ampoule under a N2 atmosphere. After evacuation and sealing, the ampoule was heated to 400 °C at a speed of 5 °C min−1. After being kept at 400 °C for 40 h, ground and sifted, the COF powder was obtained. Second, 500 mg COF powder was dispersed in an H2SO4 (98%) solution in a 250-mL three-necked round-bottom flask with stirring at room temperature for 6 h. A total of 6 mL H2O2 was added dropwise to the mixed solution after heating to 160 °C. With the appearance of smoke, the solution color turned from yellow to wine red within 5 min, indicating the formation of NCDs. The obtained reactants were purified with a combined treatment process of filtration and rotary evaporation to remove the unreacted COFs, and the final pure NCDs were obtained by freeze-drying.

COF-derived NCDs@MIPs were synthesized by normal reverse microemulsion methods. The reverse microemulsion system consisted of Triton X-100 (1.8 mL) and cyclohexane (7.5 mL). One milliliter of NCDs (0.5 μg mL−1, dispersed in water), 50 μL of TEOS, and 100 μL of ammonia were added into the system one by one, with continuous stirring for 2 h in the dark. Twenty microliters of APTES and 100 μL of FLO (2.5 mg mL−1, dispersed in methanol, acting as template molecules) were added into the above system, and the mixture solution was stirred overnight. The obtained reactant was purified by acetone and water three times under centrifugation (8000 rpm, 2 min), dried at 50 °C, ground and sieved to obtain the final COF-derived NCDs@MIPs. Prior to use of MIPs for targets, Soxhlet extraction was performed on the materials to remove the template molecules by using methanol as the solvent. The nonimprinted polymers (NIPs) were synthesized under the same conditions as above without the addition of the template molecules.

All fluorescence measurements were performed under the same conditions. One milligram of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs was mixed with a FLO standard solution (0.03–0.3 μg mL−1) for multifunctional microplate reader analysis. The excitation wavelength was set at 340 nm, and the emission was recorded over the range of 370–570 nm.

Interference and coexisting interference assays were conducted to assess the specificity of the developed COF-derived NCDs@MIPs for the target analyte. Both MIPs and NIPs were evaluated with imidacloprid (IMI), thiacloprid (THI), thiamethoxam (THIA), nitenpyram (NIT), acetamiprid (ACE), M1 (a mixture of the above five pesticides without FLO) and M2 (mixture of the above five pesticides with FLO). The concentrations of the structural analogs were the same as those of FLO, from 0.03 to 0.3 μg mL−1, and the same procedure was used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of vegetable and fruit samples
ce:section.ce:para: Both the peel and pulp of apples and cucumbers were separately used to investigate the application of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs for FLO detection. To verify the recovery accuracy, experiments were carried out with samples spiked at 0.09, 0.12, and 0.15 μg g−1. Twelve grams of samples were cut into appropriately size pieces, extracted with 60 mL of a acetonitrile/methanol solvent mixture (v/v, 8:2) and shaken for 1 h. After filtration, the obtained clear liquid was further concentrated by rotary evaporation at 60 °C, and the residue was resuspended in methanol to a final sample solution volume of 3 mL. All test solutions were prepared in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of NCDs and COF-derived NCDs@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The COF-derived NCDs@MIPs were prepared by a one-pot polymerization method with water-soluble NCDs. The major steps used in the imprinting synthesis are shown in Fig. 1 . The highly chemically stable COFs were polymerized by 1,4-dicyanobenzene and molten ZnCl2 at 400 °C (Fig. S1). Water-soluble NCDs were exfoliated from prepared COFs by the dropwise addition of a strong piranha solution owing to triazine hydrolysis and cleavage of the triazine and benzene rings. After a series of purification steps to remove any unreacted reagents, the samples were freeze-dried, and the NCD powder was obtained. The fluorescence spectrum of the NCDs in Fig. S2 show that the intensity and maximum emission wavelength depended on the excitation wavelength (from 290 to 370 nm). Then, reverse microemulsion methods were used for the synthesis of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs. In this part of the reaction, APTES was used as a functional monomer to react with FLO through noncovalent interactions, and TEOS was subsequently added to the reaction system as a cross-linker. After Soxhlet extraction for removing the template molecules (FLO), a series of three-dimensional cavities were created in the surface of the N-CQDs@MIPs with matched size, structure, and functional groups for the FLO target. The fluorescence intensity of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs was as high as 14,632 a.u., but it was quenched sharply to 6124 a.u. after target adsorption, as shown in Fig. S3. When the target molecules were then extracted from the probe, the fluorescence intensity of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs was 14,216 a.u., and it was almost restored to its original intensity. The UV–vis spectra of the extracted solution also indicated that the target molecules were almost completely extracted from COF-derived NCDs@MIPs (Fig. S4). The doping of nitrogen into carbon-based materials can cause new defects by the difference in bond length, atomic size, and coordination between carbon and nitrogen atoms, which may lead to the abundance of defect sites with local charge. The defects caused by nitrogen doping can serve as active sites and transfer charges to reactants, thus facilitating a charge transfer from NCDs to reactants. The abovementioned excellent characteristics enable nitrogen-doped carbon materials to be used as fluorescence probes.

Morphology characterizations by HRTEM shown in Fig. 2 (a) revealed that the NCDs were present in monodisperse and spherical particles with sizes less than 10 nm, and the inset shows that the lattice spacing was 0.10 nm. The characterization image scanned by SEM of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs displayed in Fig. 2(b) was approximately 50 nm larger than that of the NCDs. It was further clearly observed in Fig. 2(c) and Fig. 2(d) that the average diameters of the NCDs and COF-derived NCDs@MIPs were 3.3 nm and 41.6 nm, respectively. This result indicated that COF-derived NCDs@MIPs were synthesized successfully on the NCDs. The FT-IR spectra of the NCDs and COF-derived NCDs@MIPs are shown in Fig. 2e. From the spectrum of the NCDs, the stronger stretching vibration at 1610 cm−1 was related to the nonconjugated CN, the absorption band at 1500 cm−1 corresponded to triazine, and the peak at 3450 cm−1 was related to the OH stretching vibrations. Compared to the prepared NCDs, the peak at 1052 cm−1 was due to asymmetric stretching of the Si-O-Si, which yielded a broad absorption band, illustrating that the MIPs were successfully grafted onto the surface of the NCDs.

The UV–vis spectrum of the FLO standards and fluorescence emission spectrum of the COF-derived NCDs@MIPs are shown in Fig. 2(f). The UV maximum absorption wavelength of FLO standards was 260 nm, and the optimum fluorescence emission wavelength was 390 nm. These two peaks did not overlap, which revealed that the fluorescence quenching between COF-derived NCDs@MIPs and FLO molecules was not in accordance with the principle of energy transfer. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs may have been quenched by charge transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of solvent of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the stability of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs, the appropriate dispersion solvent was chosen by water, methanol and acetonitrile. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the fluorescence intensity of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs grew persistently in acetonitrile from 0 to 60 min; however, there was no obvious change in the fluorescence intensity of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs in water and methanol.

The adsorption solvent for FLO analysis was optimized by water, M1 (Vwater:methanol, 9:1), M2 (Vwater:methanol, 8:2), methanol, and acetonitrile. The ratio of blank and measured values (F0/F) for COF-derived NCDs@MIPs responding to FLO molecules was used as an indicator of measurement. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the value of F0/F was highest at 1.65 in water. It was obvious that COF-derived NCDs@MIPs exhibited the best response to FLO in water. Therefore, water was selected as the solvent for further study.

To improve the sensitivity of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs, the concentration of NCDs was optimized during the synthesis process. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the concentration of NCDs was chosen at 0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75 and 1.00 μg mL−1 to investigate the response of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs to FLO standards. Compared with different concentrations of NCDs, a significant difference in fluorescence intensity was observed for FLO concentrations from 0 to 0.3 μg g−1. The results were consistent with previous conclusions that the fluorescence intensity of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs decreased with increasing FLO concentrations. The slope (k value) of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs interacting with different concentrations of FLO molecules is shown in Fig. 3d. The maximum k value was obtained at an NCD concentration of 0.50 μg mL−1, revealing an optimum polymerization concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of dose of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The dose of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs seriously affected the results of the study, and COF-derived NCDs@MIPs doses of 0.25, 0.5, 1, and 2 mg were mixed with a 0.15 μg mL−1 FLO solution. As clearly observed in Fig. 3 (e), the change in the fluorescence response of the COF-derived NCDs@MIPs was obvious at doses of 1 and 2 mg. When the dose reached 2 mg, there was no marked difference in the fluorescence when compared with that of 1 mg COF-derived NCDs@MIPs. The increase in the dosage of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs did not enhance the response signal because the recognition sites were sufficient for FLO. Therefore, we chose 1 mg of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs for FLO detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on COF-derived NCDs@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The pH value, namely, that between 2 and 12, is one of the key factors affecting FLO optosensing. The fluorescence response of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs and NIPs for FLO molecules is shown in Fig. 3 (f). The best fluorescence response of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs to FLO (0.15 μg mL−1) was observed at pH = 7.0. As the pH value increased, F0/F decreased quickly because an alkaline environment can cause ionization of the silica shell. At a low pH value, the surface of the silica shell could be dissolved, producing surface defects that affect the fluorescence intensity. A similar phenomenon was observed for the COF-derived NCDs@NIPs. Although the response of COF-derived NCDs@NIPs to FLO was not as clear as that of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs, it was obviously noted that at pH = 7, the fluorescence response was consistent with that of the MIPs. Thus, pH = 7.0 was used in the subsequent studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs for FLO
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the selectivity and interference of the optosensing method, a series of concentrations of neonicotinoids were chosen to carry out the specificity experiment under optimized conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the fluorescence response of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs for FLO was higher than that of its structurally analogous neonicotinoids, including IMI, THI, THIA, NLT, and ACE. The mixture of the five kinds of analogues (M1) was subjected to COF-derived NCDs@MIPs, and the fluorescence response was not obviously changed. However, M1 mixed with a FLO standard (M2) interacted with the COF-derived NCDs@MIPs, and the fluorescence response was significantly different from that of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs subjected to FLO only. The results suggested that the analogues exhibited negligible interference in FLO analysis.

The slope (k value) of the interaction of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs and NIPs with FLO molecules and their analogues are shown in Fig. 4d to further evaluate the selectivity. Compared to the small and irregular response signal of COF-derived NCDs@NIPs for FLO and its analogues, the fluorescence response of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs was significantly different for FLO and its analogues. The ratio of the k of FLO with that of analogues was used as an indicator to measure selectivity, and it was 10.05, 6.44, 7.23, 5.13, and 10.33 for IMI, THI, THIA, NLT, and ACE, respectively, which displayed excellent selectivity and specificity owing to the specific recognition sites produced by the molecular imprinting technique. The above results demonstrated that COF-derived NCDs@MIPs could be used to detect FLO in a complex system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration curve of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs for FLO
ce:section.ce:para:
To achieve the aim of accurate and selective detection for FLO based on COF-derived NCDs@MIPs, as presented in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence intensity of the COF-derived NCDs@MIPs sharply decreased with increasing FLO standard concentrations. With the addition of FLO, the lowest fluorescence intensity was obtained with 0.30 μg g−1 FLO, which was decreased by 59.2% of the intensity in the absence of FLO. The fluorescence quenching effect of the COF-derived NCDs@MIPs towards different concentrations of FLO (CFLO, μg g−1) can be calculated by the Stern-Volmer equation as follows:
F0/F = KsvCFLO + 1(1)

where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs under FLO-free conditions and at a given related FLO concentration, respectively, Ksv is the quenching constant of the Stern-Volmer equation, and CFLO is the concentration of the FLO standard solution. The variation of F 0/F showed a linear relationship with the FLO concentration from 0.03 to 0.30 μg g−1, as seen in the insert in Fig. 5(a). The correlation coefficient of R 2 was 0.9920, and the detection limit was calculated from the FLO concentration that gave a signal-to-noise ratio of three, which was 0.0079 μg g−1. To demonstrate the imprinting effect, the response of COF-derived NCDs@NIPs to FLO molecules was investigated as a comparison group. The COF-derived NCDs@NIPs showed the same trend for FLO molecule interaction, but the extent of fluorescence quenching was very small (30.2%) for NIPs (Fig. 5b) and was significantly lower than that of COF-derived NCDs@MIPs. According to the results obtained above, COF-derived NCDs@MIPs present excellent analytical performance as a FLO detection method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of FLO based on COF-derived NCDs@MIPs in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: The COF-derived NCDs@MIPs were applied to detect FLO in cucumber and apple samples. Cucumbers and apples were purchased from a local supermarket. The peel and pulp were separated for both the cucumbers and apples. The results indicated that no FLO was detected by this method in the samples, and recovery experiments were conducted to verify the accuracy of the method with FLO at 0.09, 0.12, and 0.15 μg g−1. The recovery range for the samples ranged from 86.7% to 98.1% with a relative standard deviation range of 0.9%–5.3% (n = 3) (Table 1 ). Therefore, the N-CQDs@MIPs showed the ability to accurately measure FLO in unknown food samples, which suggests that N-CQDs@MIPs can be used to analyze real samples.

In summary, optosensing detection based on COF-derived NCDs@MIPs was developed for the highly sensitive and selective recognition of FLO. The COF-derived NCDs@MIPs were used as a fluorescence probe and were synthetized by one-pot reverse microemulsion polymerization, with the advantage of good reusability and fluorescence properties simple synthesis and the potential for batch production. Owing to the piranha solution, the fluorescence-contributing NCDs were cut from exfoliated COFs by triazine hydrolysis and bond cleavage of the triazine and benzene rings. The optosensing detection method for FLO exhibited a wide linear range from 0.03 to 0.30 μg g−1, a good linear correlation of 0.9939, and a low detection limit of 0.004 μg g−1. Furthermore, the COF-derived NCDs@MIPs prepared in this work could be used as a sensitive and selective detection method for FLO in vegetable and fruit samples.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31822040), the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2018YFC1602300), the Young Top-Notch Talent of High-Level Innovation and Entrepreneurs Support Program (2017000026833ZK28), the School Level Cultivation Fund of Beijing Technology and Business University for Distinguished and Excellent Young Scholars (BTBUYP2020), and Postgraduate Cultivation of Beijing Technology and Business University-The Improvement Plan of Postgraduate Education Quality (19008020144).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105585.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
